summary: The narrowest stable slice was to enforce `storage:*` typing in the checker and derive storage contracts from existing generated host-binding/schema metadata in `runtime/bun/server.mjs`, instead of adding new source syntax.
ergonomics: Existing host binding manifests already carried enough type/schema info to derive storage metadata without touching the parser or lowering pipeline.
The test suite structure made it easy to add one checker regression, one runtime regression, and one scenario assertion in support-console.
`cabal test --test-options` is picky here; `--pattern storage` worked, while some `-p=` forms were rejected by the test runner wrapper.
follow_ups: Emit a first-class compiler-owned `__claspStorage` export so storage metadata is available without runtime reconstruction.
Add schema-level enum value metadata if later tasks want generated `CHECK (... IN ...)` constraints for enum-backed columns.
Introduce explicit migration helpers that consume the derived table declarations instead of leaving them as contract metadata only.
warnings: `storage:*` enforcement is strict: bare `Str`/`Int`/`Bool` anywhere in the storage boundary type now fails with `E_STORAGE_BOUNDARY_TYPE`, including inside lists.
The support-console verification string now includes storage contract fields; future changes to storage metadata shape will need the example test and verify script updated together.
`bash scripts/verify-all.sh` emits a lot of dirty-tree and Node module-type warnings that are noisy but non-fatal; the actual pass/fail signal is the command exit code.
tests_run: nix develop -c bash -lc 'cabal test clasp-compiler-test --test-options="--pattern storage"'
nix develop -c bash -lc 'cabal test clasp-compiler-test --test-options="--pattern support-console"'
residual_risks: Storage table derivation currently covers record/list/enum/primitive schema shapes via runtime metadata only; there is no emitted dedicated compiler-side `__claspStorage` artifact yet.
Generated constraints are intentionally conservative: `NOT NULL`, first-field `id` primary key, boolean domain checks, and JSON validity for nested record/list storage columns.
Table naming is deterministic snake_case from schema type names, but pluralization and migration-oriented naming customization are still absent.
